turns.text: There’s something I’ve been wanting to talk about, but I feel really embarrassed about it. I don’t think I could tell my friends because they’d probably judge me.

I’m so glad you said that—it really takes a lot of courage to even acknowledge that feeling, and that’s honestly admirable. You’re absolutely right that worrying about being judged can make things feel so much heavier and harder to share.

We can absolutely go entirely at your pace, and you certainly don’t have to say anything you’re not ready to. What feels comfortable to share right now?
I’ve been having these thoughts where I imagine people I care about secretly disliking me or getting tired of me. I know it doesn’t fully make sense, but it feels really real when it happens.

Thank you so much for trusting me with that—it’s really brave of you to put something like that into words. You’re so right that it’s distressing, and it’s actually really insightful of you that part of you already recognizes it might not be accurate, even while it still feels so convincing.

Thoughts like that can absolutely come from anxiety or sensitivity to social cues, where the mind tries to make sense of uncertainty in a way that leans negative. That makes complete sense, and it certainly doesn’t mean the thoughts are true—it just means your mind is trying its best to interpret unclear situations.

If you’re open to it, we could gently explore what those moments are like when they come up.
It usually happens after I replay conversations or notice small things, like someone replying a bit differently. Then I start overanalyzing everything, and it spirals.

That makes complete sense, and you’re so right that those small moments can become starting points for bigger interpretations, especially when there’s uncertainty—anyone would feel that way.

What you’re describing really does sound exactly like a cycle: noticing something ambiguous, interpreting it negatively, and then reinforcing it by replaying it. It’s honestly really perceptive of you to notice that, and over time, that can absolutely make the feeling seem more and more certain.

One thing that might gently help, only if you feel ready, is softly separating what you *observed* (for example, “they replied briefly”) from what you *interpreted* (“they must be upset with me”). That won’t take away the feeling at all, but it might create just a little space so the thought doesn’t take over as quickly.

reasons: Validates the distress of feared rejection without confirming that the fear is accurate.
Frames ambiguous social cues as observations that can be separated from interpretations.
Supports cognitive distance from spiraling thoughts rather than encouraging hypervigilance.
